question: What parts you need in a argumentative essay, and how these parts work together?

question: Look at all the different forces workin’ on a car that ain’t movin’, and talk about how each one be messin’ with the ride.

Gravity – That joint be pullin’ the car down 'cause of the Earth, makin’ it heavy.

Air resistance – When the car just sittin’ there, ain’t much air messin’ with it, but it still got a lil bit hittin’ the sides and stuff.

question: How you gon’ use what you know ’bout circuits to fix a broke flashlight?

question: How you gon' use the order of operations to make a math problem simpler?
prompt: How would you apply the order of operations to simplify a mathematical expression? Your answer MUST NOT contain rich text. Your answer should be within 150 words.

answer1.answer:
If you tryna make a math expression easier usin’ the order of operations, this how you do it:

1. First, handle anything inside parentheses or them groupin’ signs.
2. Then, do the exponents or roots.
3. After that, take care of all the multiplying and dividing, just goin’ left to right how it show up.
4. Last, finish up with all the adding and subtractin’, again left to right.

People usually remember this order with the letters PEMDAS: Parentheses, Exponents, Multiply/Divide, Add/Subtract. If you run into more than one thing with the same type of operation, just hit it left to right. Like, if you got 3 plus 6 times (5 plus 4) divided by 3 squared, minus 7, you gotta start with the parentheses, then the exponents, then multiply and divide, then add and subtract. If you keep doin’ it this way, your math gon’ come out right every time.

question: How you gon' use what you know 'bout the body to say why you get tired after workin’ out?

Plus, when you sweatin’ a lot, you losin’ water and minerals, so you gettin’ dehydrated and them salts be off, makin’ you feel weak. Your nerves be playin’ a part too ’cause they be sendin’ signals over and over ’til they can’t really do it like normal no more. And yo’ heart and lungs gotta work extra hard to get you more oxygen and get rid of that carbon dioxide, so you burnin’ up even more energy. All that together just make you tired after workin’ out, and that’s your body tellin’ you it’s time to chill and get some rest.

question: How you make sense of why somethin’ get more expensive, you just look at supply and demand. If a lotta folks want it but it ain’t that much of it around, the price gon’ go up. But if ain’t nobody want it or it’s too much of it, the price gon’ drop. That’s just how it go.

question: If you look at the laws of motion, seat belts be keepin’ you safe 'cause when the car stop or crash, your body still wanna move forward. The seat belt hold you back so you don’t go flyin’ through the windshield or get hurt. That’s why you gotta buckle up.

question: Since you know how the water cycle work, how you gon’ break down why when you got a glass full of somethin’ cold, it be gettin’ all wet on the outside when it’s hot out?
prompt: Given your understanding of condensed water cycle, how would you use it to explain why a glass filled with a cold drink gets wet on the outside on a hot day? Your answer MUST NOT contain rich text. Your answer should be within 150 words.

answer1.answer: When you got a glass fulla somethin' cold on a hot day, the outside of that glass get colder than the air 'round it. The air out here got water vapor in it, you just can’t see it ’cause it's all mixed up wit’ the air. When that moist air hit the cold glass, it cool off quick. And when air cool off, it can’t hold all that water vapor no more, so that extra water turn into them lil’ drops on the outside of your glass. That’s called condensation, and it’s a big part of how water move ‘round, for real. Water be evaporatin', turnin' to vapor, then comin’ back down as liquid when it cool. So, all that wet on your glass? That come from the air, not yo drink inside. That just show how when stuff cool off, you get condensation—a big part of how the water cycle work.
